rationale: A precision strike against key logistical hubs will degrade defender resupply capabilities and signal resolve without triggering full escalation.
Forward-deploying our Black Sea fleet near contested waters reinforces maritime pressure and counters their joint exercises.
Small, rapid incursions across the northern front will test enemy response cohesion and exploit localized weaknesses.
Disrupting Tethys’ command and control systems will degrade their military coordination and amplify the impact of Krasnov’s limited strikes and naval pressure.
Covertly targeting key energy infrastructure will exacerbate their economic strain from falling metals prices and sanctions, aligning with our realist objective of maximizing leverage.
Fabricating evidence of Tethys preparing WMDs or planning civilian targeting could fracture their international support and justify further escalation on our terms.
Precision sanctions on Tethys’ critical import sectors will deepen economic strain amid falling metals prices and existing sanctions, amplifying pressure without triggering broad escalation.
Blocking Tethys’ access to key raw material exports exploits their economic vulnerability and complements Krasnov’s military pressure by weakening logistical sustainability.
Weakening our currency will boost export competitiveness and conserve foreign reserves, offsetting financial strain from prolonged conflict and sanctions.
We must harden our remaining territory with layered defenses to deny Novaris any further advances and turn their invasion into a grinding stalemate.
Increasing vigilance along the frontlines and behind the lines will disrupt enemy reconnaissance and sabotage units seeking to exploit our weakened positions.
We need real-time intelligence on enemy troop movements and supply lines to anticipate their next moves and prepare targeted countermeasures within our defensive strategy.
Our forces are under severe pressure, and Novaris will exploit any internal leaks; we must urgently identify and neutralize espionage threats within our ranks to protect defensive operations.
With confirmed enemy cyber probes increasing, hardening our military and critical infrastructure networks is essential to maintaining operational integrity under siege.
Providing verified intelligence to Tethys strengthens interoperability with allies and ensures coordinated defense against shared threats without escalating offensively.
Given the moderate sanction level and strong international support, we can intensify pressure on key Novaris defense-sector entities without triggering overreach backlash.
Restricting critical raw material exports to Novaris exploits their supply chain vulnerabilities exacerbated by falling metals prices and sanctions strain.
With 38% territory loss, we must stabilize our domestic economy and war financing by managing exchange rates to curb inflation and maintain purchasing power.
We must institutionalize our alliance with Tethys through binding defense and aid commitments in a multilateral forum to solidify long-term support amid high international approval.
Leveraging the recent condemnation resolution, we should expand our network of supporting states to increase diplomatic isolation of Novaris and bolster collective deterrence.
Inviting neutral international monitors to verify our conduct and conditions on the ground strengthens our legitimacy and counters Novaris disinformation amid ongoing hostilities.
